old_claim.note: Mining provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct mining edge remains because the corrected node models chemical knowledge and experimental use of light-sensitive silver salts, not raw silver extraction or ore-supply infrastructure.

source_shape.source_locator: Litchfield reprint of Davy's 1802 Account, pp. 189-194: the account describes use of nitrate of silver in prepared media and experiments with light exposure, not mining, ore processing, or silver supply chains.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports a laboratory silver-salt photochemistry node and does not make silver mining a direct prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Mined silver is remote material provenance for many silver compounds, but the graph edge should represent the source-backed capability boundary of this chemistry node.

ontology_before: Silver-salt photochemistry depended directly on Mining, implying ore extraction enabled the first-known chemical understanding or image experiments.
ontology_after: Silver-salt photochemistry is constrained to laboratory chemistry; raw-material extraction is not modeled as a direct prerequisite for this knowledge node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: mining is no longer a direct prerequisite for silver_nitrate_chemistry.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to industrial production of silver nitrate from mined silver at scale.
A stronger source showed the first-known silver-nitrate photochemical experiments depended on a specific mining technology.
A separate silver-nitrate-manufacture node were added where silver supply chains are the direct subject.
short_reason: Mining is remote material provenance, not a direct dependency for silver-salt photochemistry.
